title: EECS16A: What I Actually Learned About Signals
preview: Attending Prof. Babak Ayazifar's lectures changed how I think about signals. They are not just math — they are structured data you can measure, compare, and use to build real things.

detail.body:
I had the chance to attend lectures by Professor Babak Ayazifar, and the way he framed signals made everything click differently. Signals are not just waveforms. Once you represent them as vectors, the whole toolkit of linear algebra becomes available.

The core idea that kept coming back: a signal x of length n is just a point in n-dimensional space.

x = [x₁  x₂  ···  xₙ]ᵀ

From there, comparing signals is a geometry problem.

For voice recognition, the lab aligned recordings, vectorized them, then used SVD to find the directions of most variation. Projecting into that lower-dimensional space and classifying by nearest centroid is surprisingly effective.

projected = Uᵀ · x
predicted class = argmin‖x − cₖ‖

Real-life example: think of Shazam. When you hold your phone up to a song, it does not compare the full audio. It extracts a fingerprint — the strongest peaks in the spectrogram — and matches those against a database. A 3-minute song becomes a few dozen (time, frequency) pairs. Fast, compact, and robust to noise.

The spectrogram comes from applying the DFT over short overlapping windows:

X(k) = Σ x(n) · e^(−j2πkn/N)

Each window gives you frequency content at that moment. Stack them and you get a 2D picture of how a sound evolves. The raw waveform hides timing. The spectrogram shows it.

The acoustic positioning lab was the most satisfying. Speakers play tones, a microphone hears them with a delay, and cross-correlation finds that delay precisely.

R(τ) = Σ x(n) · y(n + τ)

Peak of R(τ) gives Δt. Then d = v · Δt where v is 343 m/s. With three beacons, three distance estimates, the position is a least-squares solve:

Ax = b   →   x̂ = A⁺b

I thought positioning required GPS. It just requires correlation and geometry.

media.caption: Voice recognition: from raw audio to nearest-centroid classification
Waveform to spectrogram to compact fingerprint
Acoustic positioning: cross-correlation, delay estimation, localization
